coredata.dc:title: A user-secure and highly selective enhancement of latent fingerprints by magnetic composite powder based on carbon dot fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Lifeng
Peng, Di
Wang, Ruonan
Li, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: A novel bifunctional composite powder with moderate magnetic properties and intense fluorescence was prepared via a layer-by-layer assembly route. After the self-assembly procedure run up to 7 times, the as-prepared Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) began to overcome the quenching effect from Fe3O4 nanoparticles and shown intense blue emission (λem = 434 nm) under 370 nm UV irradiation with a quantum yield of 45.04%. The morphological characterization revealed that it has a spherical and uniform core-shell structure of approximately 120 nm in diameter. The component analysis and contact angle test manifested the hydrophilic groups on the surface of the final products. As a result, this novel nanocomposite powder has two advantages over traditional fingerprint powder in both morphology/size and organic functional groups, which could provide more suitable physical adsorption and more chemical bonding in the interaction between fingerprint residues and powders. A series of fingerprint enhancement experiments and a novel quantitative evaluation both confirmed its high contrast, high sensitivity, and especially high selectivity over traditional methods for latent fingerprints. More importantly, in addition to its saturation magnetization of 6.4 emu/g, the abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of our composite powder also played an important role in absorbing moisture in air and print residues to avoid powder dust from the user’s respiratory tract. Therefore, a fast, user-secure, and highly selective method was established and expected to be valuable in forensic science and related fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Latent fingerprint
Carbon dot
Hydrophilic interaction
Forensic science
Fluorescence
Nanoparticle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Layer-by-layer assembly of Fe3O4@SiO2 and CD solutions
Application of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) for latent fingerprint enhancement

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( NSFC51604180 ), Key Research and Development Program of Shanxi Province ( 201903D321068 ), Major and Key Science and Technology Research Program of Chongqing Education Commission ( KJZD-M202000301 , KJZD201800301 ), Cultivate Scientific Research Excellence Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi ( CSREP2019KJ038 ), and Key Research Project of Southwest University of Political Science and Law ( 2018XZZD-07 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic view of the hydrophilic interaction between Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder and fingerprint residue.
FTIR spectra of pure CDs (a), Fe3O4 NPs (b), silica-encapsulated Fe3O4 (c), and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD (n) (d).
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Fe3O4 NPs (a) (inset: HRTEM image of a representative particle), Fe3O4@SiO2 (b), Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) (c) and high-resolution Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) (d).

Photoluminescence spectrum of CD solution (a) (inset: image of fluorescent CD) and emission spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powders (b) (right: photographs of luminescent powders gradually increasing from bottom to top). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Zeta potentials of products at different stages. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Hysteresis loops of Fe3O4 NPs (a), Fe3O4@SiO2 (b) and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) (c). The inset shows images of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder with a magnetic brush before and after 365 nm light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Developed fingerprint images of BMP (a), CFP (b) and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder (c). Magnified images of LEVEL 2 (d: by CFP, f: by Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder) and magnified images of LEVEL 3 (e: by CFP, g: by Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder), where red circles indicate sweat pores. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Developed fingerprint images of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder on plastic sheets (a), painted wood (b), ceramic tiles (c), aluminum alloys (d), printed paper (e), cardboard (f), coins (g) and transparent glass (h) upon 365 nm irradiation in a dark field.
Comparison of developing selectivity of three methods.

head.ce:title: A user-secure and highly selective enhancement of latent fingerprints by magnetic composite powder based on carbon dot fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology, Taiyuan 030008, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, North University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MicroNano System Research Center, College of Information and Computer & Key Laboratory of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of Ministry of Education and Shanxi Province, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MicroNano System Research Center, College of Information and Computer & Key Laboratory of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of Ministry of Education and Shanxi Province, Taiyuan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MicroNano System Research Center, College of Information and Computer & Key Laboratory of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of Ministry of Education and Shanxi Province, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel bifunctional composite powder with moderate magnetic properties and intense fluorescence was prepared via a layer-by-layer assembly route. After the self-assembly procedure run up to 7 times, the as-prepared Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) began to overcome the quenching effect from Fe3O4 nanoparticles and shown intense blue emission (λem = 434 nm) under 370 nm UV irradiation with a quantum yield of 45.04%. The morphological characterization revealed that it has a spherical and uniform core-shell structure of approximately 120 nm in diameter. The component analysis and contact angle test manifested the hydrophilic groups on the surface of the final products. As a result, this novel nanocomposite powder has two advantages over traditional fingerprint powder in both morphology/size and organic functional groups, which could provide more suitable physical adsorption and more chemical bonding in the interaction between fingerprint residues and powders. A series of fingerprint enhancement experiments and a novel quantitative evaluation both confirmed its high contrast, high sensitivity, and especially high selectivity over traditional methods for latent fingerprints. More importantly, in addition to its saturation magnetization of 6.4 emu/g, the abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of our composite powder also played an important role in absorbing moisture in air and print residues to avoid powder dust from the user’s respiratory tract. Therefore, a fast, user-secure, and highly selective method was established and expected to be valuable in forensic science and related fields.

ce:section.ce:para: For over a century, worldwide law enforcement has placed a high premium on the value of fingerprint evidence in terms of individual recognition and forensic analysis. For the purpose of criminal investigation, enhancing the visibility of latent fingerprints or other human traces is always at the top of the collection of physical evidence at crime scenes. As a result, a variety of approaches for latent fingerprint enhancement have been developed by forensic scientists, including regular powder, fluorescent powder, cyanoacrylate fuming, ninhydrin method, 1,8-diazafluoren-9-one method, and small particle reagent method. In consideration of its portability, high efficiency and easy operation, powder dusting is the most pervasive method for enhancing latent fingerprints worldwide [1,2]. Although the heat of rare earth-based powders represented by down-conversion [3–8], up-conversion [9–11] and long afterglow nanophosphors [12,13] has lasted for several years in forensic science, the dust blowing issue of the powder dusting method has been denounced for a long time, which poses a potential threat to the health of users. Accordingly, magnetic powders, which can be used to control the amount of powder with the aid of magnetic brushes, are generally considered one of the most effective means to avoid or slash dust blowing risk. Unfortunately, traditional magnetic powders for latent fingerprint enhancement are usually nonfluorescent, which curbs the ridge signal and thus the contrast of fingerprints, especially on multicolor substrates or textures. In addition, commercial fluorescent powders are mostly hybrids and usually contain organic dyes as luminescent ingredients and other regular powders to maintain the flowability for powder brushing. Consequently, the huge discrepancy in particle shape and size of the hybrid powders could play a negative role in the adhesion of powders and fingerprint residues, eventually cutting down the detection sensitivity as well as selectivity of latent fingerprints. Therefore, there is an urgent requirement for developing a novel powder with multifunctionality, low toxicity, and high effectiveness in forensic science.

It is a long-standing topic to protect the health of users by utilizing the controllability of superparamagnetic powder such as Fe3O4 or other iron oxide nanoparticles. In recent years, quantum dots (QDs), noble metal nanoclusters (NMNCs) and rare earth luminescent materials (RELMs) with magnetic properties have been reported as effective probes for latent fingerprint enhancement [14,15] and many other scopes, such as bioimaging [16], immunoassays [17–19], magnetic separation [20–22], drug transport [23–25] and ion detection [26,27]. Nevertheless, the toxicity of cadmium and the high cost of noble metals or rare earths still hinder the practical application of QDs, NMNCs or RELMs in crime scenes.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), which are well known for their excellent optical properties, facile surface modification, low toxicity, and low cost of raw materials [28], have shown a great deal of interesting applications in bioimaging [29–32], medical therapy [33–36], photocatalysis [37–39], photodevices [40–43], detecting & sensors [44–46] and light communication [47,48] and so on. Since the first application of CD-based nanopowders for fingerprint recovery was reported by D. Fernandes and his coworkers [49,50], many followers have successfully employed CD-based solutions [51–53] or powders [54–58] for detecting latent fingerprints in the last several years. On the one hand, CD-based solutions for latent fingerprint enhancement usually require longer incubation times, which makes it difficult to meet the needs of on-site rapid processing. On the other hand, because the synthesis method of high solid-state fluorescence of CDs in powdery form at present is still limited, CD-based powders used in latent fingerprint enhancement are nonmagnetic, which still remains a risk to use health. Accordingly, although these newly emerging strategies have exhibited potential in forensic science, there are no reports about magnetic fluorescent CDs and their user-secure application in latent fingerprint enhancement.

Herein, an ingenious and fast synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder possessing moderate magnetic and photoluminescence characteristics was proposed. The negatively charged CDs were prepared using gelatin as the carbon source and ethanediol as the dispersing agent, which exhibited bright blue emission under 365 nm irradiation. To endow the as-prepared CDs with magnetism, a layer-by-layer assembly route was devised using a cationic surfactant as an electrostatic adsorbent for binding the negatively charged CDs together. The fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder were basically comparable with those of pure CDs. The moderate magnetism of this bifunctional product enabled magnetic separation in the process of synthesis and fingerprint detection with a magnetic brush. Moreover, the abundant carboxyl and hydroxy groups as well as the negative charge on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) provide potentially enhanced covalent or electrostatic binding between fingerprint residues and the composite powder ( Fig. 1). Therefore, an ingenious and fast synthetic route was presented for CD-based composite powder combining high fluorescence and moderate magnetic properties, which has been demonstrated as a user-secure and efficient approach for latent fingerprint enhancement.

Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2•4H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3•6H2O), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), and polydimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride (PDDA) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd., China. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), concentrated ammonia (NH3•H2O, 25～28%), sodium chloride (NaCl), gelatin, ethanediol, anhydrous ethanol, and isopropyl alcohol were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. All reagents were of analytical grade, and deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-F200, Japan) was used for morphological characterization. Scanning electron microscope (Gemini SEM 450, Zeiss, Germany) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were employed to detect the content changes of C, Fe, Si, N and O elements between Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n). The chemical composition was recorded by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, MAGNA-IR550 II, USA) using KBr pellets as the sample matrix and X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). The contact angle was obtained by the JC2000D series Shanghai Zhongchen POWEREACH. The photoluminescence property of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) was studied by an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., LTD., Japan). A vibrating sample magnetometer (SQUID-VSM, MPMS-3, Quantum Design, USA) was used for hysteresis loops, and the zeta potentials of the CD solution, Fe3O4@SiO2, PDDA-modified Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) NPs were examined by a Zetasizer Nano ZS ZEN3600 (Malvern Inst. Ltd., UK). For the purpose of comparison, a SEM test was carried out to detect the morphologies of black magnetic powder and commercial fluorescent powder.

Magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (MFNPs) were prepared by a modified coprecipitation method previously reported [59]. In a typical process, 5.2 g FeCl3•6H2O and 2.0 g FeCl2•4H2O were mixed in 100 mL degassed deionized water with consistent mechanical stirring under a nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, concentrated ammonia was added dropwise to the mixed solution to maintain the pH around 9. After the solution turned from brown yellow to quite black, the MFNPs were finally collected by magnetic separation and then washed with anhydrous ethanol and deionized water several times to remove redundant byproducts .

In short, 40 mg MFNPs were added to a mixture of 40 mL deionized water and 200 mL isopropyl alcohol. Then, the mixture was placed in an ultrasonic bath until evenly dispersed. After that, 7 mL NH3•H2O and 0.6 mL TEOS were added dropwise to the mixture and stirred at 30 °C for 9 h to form Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 NPs. The final product could be obtained by magnetic separation, washed several times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, and freeze dried for 12 h.

The CD solution could be obtained by a hydrothermal synthesis. Typically, 0.5 g gelatin was added to 40 mL ethanediol and dissolved at 75 °C. The mixed solution was then transferred to a TFR100-Teflon reaction kettle and heated for 2 h at 200 ℃. After the reactor cooled to room temperature, the solution was mixed with 60 mL deionized water, and yellowish brown CD solution was eventually obtained, which was preserved in the dark for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Layer-by-layer assembly of Fe3O4@SiO2 and CD solutions
ce:section.ce:para: In the layer-by layer assembly of Fe3O4@SiO2 and CDs, Tris and NaCl were first dissolved in deionized water to prepare a 20 mmol/L buffer solution with the pH adjusted by HCl at 9. After that, 0.012 g Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 was added to 20 mL PDDA with buffer solution (0.2% PDDA, 20 mmol/L Tris and NaCl, pH=9), and then ultrasound treatment was performed for 30 min. Subsequently, PDDA-modified Fe3O4@SiO2 was collected by a magnetic field and washed with the above buffer solution. Afterwards, it was dispersed to 3 mL CD solution to allow positively charged Fe3O4@SiO2-NH2 to electrostatically adsorb negatively charged CD solution by shaking for 3 h. Finally, the above procedure proceeded repeatedly until the required Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) composite nanopowders were available (n = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11).

The latent fingerprint samples were from different volunteers and prepared by lightly rubbing the fingers on their noses and foreheads before touching the surface. Various substrates, including plastic sheets, painted wood, ceramic tiles, aluminum alloys, printed paper, cardboard, coins and transparent glass, were selected for depositing latent fingerprints. The powder dusting method was employed to develop the latent fingerprints using a magnetic brush. In contrast, some traditional development methods, e.g., black magnetic powder and commercial fluorescent powder, were chosen as controls. The fingerprint images were observed under daylight or 365 nm irradiation detection and then recorded with a digital camera (EDS 6D, Canon, Japan). More ridge details were captured by a digital microscope (VHX-6000, Keyence, Japan). Finally, an automatic fingerprint identification system (AFIS 7.0, Eastern Golden Finger Technology Beijing Co., Ltd, China) was used to evaluate the minutia features of the developed fingerprints.
An automatic fingerprint identification system (AFIS 7.0, Eastern Golden Finger Technology Beijing Co., Ltd, China) was used to evaluate the minutia features of the developed fingerprints.

More importantly, the high selectivity of our Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) was proven by comparison with black magnetic powder (BMP) and commercial fluorescent powder (CFP) based on gray curve analysis and integral calculation of ridges and furrows of fingerprints. This process consisted of the following three steps.

The color fingerprint image was converted to a gray image to ensure that the gray value corresponding to the ridge was higher than that of the furrow. Otherwise, a negative digital image should be transformed if necessary. Then, ImageJ software was used to open the gray image of the fingerprint, which was divided into four zones. In each zone, the straight line tool was used to cross the ridges and furrows in a single zone. The direction of the crossing lines was kept perpendicular to the ridges, and the number of ridges and furrows crossed should be more than ten as far as possible (Fig. S8). After that, the Plot Profile tool under the Analyze menu was used to obtain the gray curves between the crossed papillary ridges and the furrows, where the crest referred to the ridge and the trough to the small furrow (Fig. S9).

The gray curve was then imported into Origin 8.5, and the crest and trough were divided one by one based on the gray curves. Afterwards, the corresponding peak area value can be obtained by integrating the crest and trough with the integration tool.

According to the peak area values of the crest and trough, the formula of local selectivity for latent fingerprint enhancement is described as follows:
(1)
Local Selectivity
=
S
C
S
T
=
∑
i = 1
n
S
Ci
∑
i = 1
n
S
Ti
where SC/SCi and ST/STi are the total/single areas of crest and rough, respectively. N is the number of local ridges and furrows.

Therefore, the total selectivity for latent fingerprint enhancement is calculated below:
(2)
Total Selectivity
=
∑
i = 1
m
Local Selectivity
i
m
where m is the number of crossing lines.

Regular powders are mostly inorganic; thus, their forensic applications are practically limited for adhering aged fingerprints or some prints on certain substrates due to the scarcity of active functional groups. Therefore, powder dusting is only considered a physical method based on weak interactions between powders and moisture in fingerprint residues in most cases. To confirm the surface groups and composition of our as-prepared products, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of Fe3O4 NPs, silica-encapsulated Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) and pure CDs was performed, as shown in Fig. 2. The peak at 572 cm−1 was attributed to Fe–O bonds (Fig. 2a), while the asymmetric stretching vibration of Si–OH newly emerged at 1,103 cm−1 (Fig. 2b), indicating the successful introduction of a silica shell onto the Fe3O4 NPs. In addition, the two peaks at 1,651 and 1,597 cm−1 of our Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) demonstrated the formation of amide I and amide II [54,60] (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, the broad absorption peaks at 3,400 cm−1 and two weak peaks contributing to the stretching vibration of hydroxy groups of carboxyl groups between 3,000～2,700 cm−1 confirmed the fact that there were plenty of carboxyls on the surface of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) [14]. The hydrophilia of this CD-coated composite nanoparticle was deeply confirmed by a contact angle test as presented in Fig. S1. As a result, the FTIR spectra and contact angle value both uncovered an abundance of hydrophilic groups on Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n), which could not only enhance the interactions with moisture or other organic ingredients in human sweat [61] but also absorb water in air to prevent dust from flying to users’ respiratory tracts.

To further verify the formation of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out to analyze the changes in element composition and chemical bonding from Fe3O4@SiO2 to Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n). On the one hand, compared with those of Fe3O4@SiO2, the survey spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) exhibited newly emerged nitrogen and higher amounts of carbon as well as lower amounts of oxygen, iron and silicon (Fig. S2a and S2b). On the other hand, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. S2d) of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) showed three typical kinds of carbon atoms, including graphitic and aliphatic (285.29 eV, C˭C and C–C), oxygenated (288.35 eV, C˭O), and nitrous (286.53 eV, C–N), indicating the classic structures of fluorescent carbon dots. Additionally, the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s, Si 2p and N 1s also suggested the existence of N–C, N–H, Si–O, C˭C and C–C bonds (Figs. S2f, S1h, S1i).

The shape and size of the particles also have significant impacts on the physical adsorption of fingerprint powders. Generally, nanoparticles with spherical morphology adhere better than those with micron size or irregular shape in the powder dusting method [62]. As shown in Fig. 3a, the TEM image suggested that the Fe3O4 NPs were spherically shaped with an average diameter of approximately 30 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of an Fe3O4 particle (inset of Fig. 3a) revealed that the distance between two adjacent planes was 0.29 nm, which was in line with the (220) plane in the cubic structure of ferric oxide. After silica coating, the product exhibited an obvious core-shell structure, and the diameter increased to approximately 50 nm (Fig. 3b). Successively, as shown in Fig. 3c and 3d, the final particles remained spherical and uniform in size, with a much larger diameter of approximately 120 nm on average, which indicated that the CDs were loaded on the surface of the shell successfully. The particle size distribution of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD (7) were exhibited in Fig. S3. Besides, the results of SEM elemental mapping and elemental analysis comparing with Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) could also prove the outer layer of CDs visually (Fig. S4) and quantitatively (Table S1). Moreover, HRTEM of the CD solution was further performed, and lattice fringe spacings of 0.36 nm and 0.25 nm were close to the (002) and (1120) facets of graphitic carbon, respectively, as shown in Fig. S5 [57,63], indicating the graphite structure of the as-prepared crystalline core. Consequently, the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) was well optimized morphologically and expected to possess enhanced physical adsorption for fingerprint residues.

To enhance the developing contrast of the fingerprint, the fluorescence of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) should be high enough. Fig. 4a exhibited the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as-prepared CDs in water. Strong blue emission (λem = 434 nm) was detected, as shown in the inset, when the excitation wavelength was determined at 370 nm. For the solid-state fluorescence of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) (Fig. 4b), a weak emission was observed when n ≤ 5, indicating that despite the presence of a silicon layer, Fe3O4 still had a quenching effect on the CD luminescence nearby. As is known, the fluorescence of CDs or other quantum dots can be signiﬁcantly suppressed by Fe3+. It is widely reported [64,65] that the oxidation-reduction reaction (ORR) between the CDs and Fe3+, the inner filter effect (IFE) as well as Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) are possibly in charge of the fluorescence quenching. More interestingly, the emission spectra of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) manifested that when n ≥ 7, their blue fluorescence became obviously visible under 370 nm irradiation, which was a commonly used forensic light source in crime scenes. Overall, as the number of CDs repeatedly coated increased, the blue intensity of the prepared nanoparticles increased, which could also be checked by the naked eye, as shown in the photographs in the right column of Fig. 4. This distance-dependent fluorescence quenching phenomenon was once verified by G. Schneider [66]. However, considering that the bigger the number of assembly times, the larger the particle size would be, it probably caused the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect of fluorescent CDs. Apart from that, the oversized particle of final product could reduce the developing sensitivity of latent fingerprints. Therefore, Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(7) was eventually selected as the optimal formula for the fingerprint application.

In addition, the change in zeta potentials of different products also revealed the different surface coatings, indicating the success of the layer-by-layer assembly process, as shown in Fig. 5. Furthermore, the solid-state fluorescence quantum yield (SFQY) of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) was detected and calculated to be as high as 45.04% when n = 7, showing intense blue emission (More details were provided in Table S2). Therefore, our Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) had excellent solid-state fluorescence, which is critical for developing fingerprint contrast.

Under the control of the magnetic brush, the magnetic powder can largely avoid the powder floating in the air, thus protecting the health of the operator in the powder dusting method. The magnetic properties of Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) were characterized over the range of magnetic fields between − 30,000 and 30,000 Oe. Classic S-type modes were presented, which manifested the superparamagetism of ferroferric oxide nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 6, the saturation magnetization of Fe3O4 NPs reached as high as 51.3 emu/g, compared with 21.4 emu/g after silica coating and 6.4 emu/g of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n), indicating that the silicon shell and CD outer layers have a diamagnetic effect on the Fe3O4 core. Nevertheless, when a magnetic brush approached our Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n), it can be observed that the powder was attracted and gathered by the magnet, as well as bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation (inset of Fig. 6). In additional, the coercivity (Hc = 108.7 Oe) and retentivity values (Mr = 0.6 emu/g) of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(7) were presented. Therefore, it was proven that the final product had moderate magnetism and intense fluorescence and can be an ideal fingerprint powder for user’s health.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) for latent fingerprint enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: From macro to micro, the quality of fingerprint images is generally evaluated based on three levels of features: [67] Level 1 (Classified Feature), Level 2 (Minutia Feature) and Level 3 (Pore & Wrinkle Feature). To demonstrate the effectiveness of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) for latent fingerprint enhancement, a black plastic sheet was chosen as the substrate, while black magnetic powder (BMP) and commercial fluorescent powder (CFP) were chosen as the controls. BMP is nonfluorescent; thus, it can only be used for prints on white or light-colored surfaces. Otherwise, it could result in a low developing contrast ( Fig. 7a). When CFP and Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) were used, the developing contrasts were both satisfactory, and well-defined minutiae (Level 2) could be achieved (Fig. 7b and 7c). However, when checked at a higher resolution, the sweat pore feature can only be available using Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n), showing a higher developing sensitivity than using CFP for fingerprint enhancement. Additionally, as can be seen in Fig. 7d and 7e, background staining was detected when the CFP method was applied, whereas there was hardly any contamination in the region of furrows by our Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder, indicating a higher developing selectivity of the novel material (Fig. 7f and 7g). The high developing selectivity could also be proven by three-dimensional (3D) imaging at different angles, as shown in Fig. S6. Therefore, this bifunctional nanoparticle exhibited high-quality images in Levels 2 and 3, which was expected to be an ideal probe for latent fingerprint enhancement.

To further investigate the applicability of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder in latent fingerprint enhancement, plastic sheet, painted wood, ceramic tile, aluminum alloy, printed paper, cardboard, coin and transparent glass were chosen as on various substrates. As shown in Fig. 8, the bifunctional powder exhibited clear ridge details and sharp edges on common surfaces in crime scenes, indicating the wide applicability of Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder.

Discussions were continually strived for the high effectiveness of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder for latent fingerprint enhancement. As mentioned before, the powder dusting method is commonly a physical phenomenon that mainly depends on physical adherence between print residues and powders. First, the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder was spherical, uniform and nanosized, which were all positive factors for suitable physical adsorption. In addition, the as-prepared powder was full of organic groups, such as carboxyls and amidogens on the particle surface, which enabled the powders to possess additional chemical bonding to some ingredients of print residues, such as water, lactic acids, and amino acids. In contrast, most commercial finger powders are hardly optimized in shape and size, which contributes to low sensitivity and inferior selectivity in recognizing fingermarks in detail. To illustrate this, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis was implemented, and as shown in Figs. S7 and S8, the aforementioned BMP and CFP were both irregularly shaped and beyond the micron scale, as expected, which leaded to poor physical adhesion between particles and finger residues. Overall, the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder contains suitable physical adsorption, enhanced chemical bonding and moderate magnetic properties and was proven to be an efficient probe for fresh and aged fingerprints without inhalation risk by users.

For years, more interest from forensic investigators has focused on the novelty of developing reagents and methods rather than on the evaluation methodology of developing effects for latent fingerprint enhancement. Currently, qualitative evaluation to describe the effectiveness of developing methods, which lacks scientific rigor, is widely used in the related literature. Notably, AFIS is a powerful tool for evaluating fingerprint image quality based on Level 2 features. To quantitatively investigate the effectiveness of the novel powder for aged fingerprints, AFIS was performed to count the number of fingerprint minutiae where BMP and CFP were used as the controls. Fig. S9 revealed the effect of the above 3 methods on the 7-d latent fingerprints on transparent glasses. Unsurprisingly, the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder exhibited as high as 70 minutiae, which was much higher than 45 for CFP and 30 for BMP under the same conditions.

Unlike AIFS, which primarily detects fingerprint minutiae, a simple and quantitative evaluation method based on developing selectivity was designed as described in 2.5.1～2.5.3. Developing selectivity refers to the specific adherence of the developing reagents to papillary ridges in latent fingerprint enhancement. In other words, ideal reagents tend to combine with ridges (finger residues) instead of with fingerprint furrows (substrate background). In other words, the larger the area of ridges is, the higher the developing selectivity of fingerprint reagents under identical conditions. Inspired by this idea [68], an ingenious quantitative method for evaluating selectivity could be established by analyzing and comparing the gray images of developed fingerprints. After being analyzed by the crossing lines (Fig. S10) and gray curves (Fig. S11) of the developed fingerprints in ImageJ software, the sequence of these three methods according to the calculated total selectivity was Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n)>CFP>BMP, which agreed with the AFIS analysis as well as visual observation results. Therefore, the as-prepared bifunctional material has been demonstrated to be highly selective not only visually but also theoretically. Such a high selectivity was both due to the suitable physical adsorption capacity and related to the chemical bonds caused by surface hydrophilic groups ( Table 1).

In summary, a layer-by-layer assembly route of magnetic fluorescent carbon dots was presented, and the final product was beyond 45.04% SFQY, 120 nm particle size, 34° contact angle and 6.4 emu/g saturation magnetization. The maximum emission of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder was located at 434 nm under 370 nm irradiation, and a distance-dependent fluorescence quenching effect was observed as the assembly number increased. Moreover, abundant carboxyl, amidogen groups and the hydrophilia were further confirmed by FTIR, XPS and contact angle measurements. More importantly, the novel composite powder was successfully applied as an ideal fluorescent probe for latent fingerprint enhancement with high-quality images in Level 2 and Level 3, indicating suitable physical adsorption and enhanced chemical bonding between print residues and developing powders. The AFIS also proved the superiority of the Fe3O4@SiO2-CD(n) powder over some traditional powders. Significantly, the abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of the as-prepared bifunctional nanopowder enabled more adsorption of water in air to avoid fingerprint powder spreading to the users’ respiratory tract. In particular, a novel strategy for quantitatively evaluating the developing selectivity of latent fingerprint enhancement was proposed and employed to prove the superiority of the magnetic fluorescent carbon dots over traditional fingerprint powders. Therefore, owing to its excellent safety, superparamagnetic fluorescence performance, and low cost, it can not only be used as an efficient probe for latent fingerprint enhancement in forensic science but also has promising prospects for bioimaging or cell separation and detection.

Dr. Peng Di is the corresponding author and in charge of the manuscript. He is also the designer and writer of this paper. Dr. Ding Lifeng made most of the synthesis and characterization of the materials. The postgraduate student Wang Ruonan done the fingerprint experiments and Dr. Li Qiang polished the English of this paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC51604180), Key Research and Development Program of Shanxi Province (201903D321068), Major and Key Science and Technology Research Program of Chongqing Education Commission (KJZD-M202000301, KJZD201800301), Cultivate Scientific Research Excellence Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (CSREP2019KJ038), and Key Research Project of Southwest University of Political Science and Law (2018XZZD-07).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2020.158160.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
